Facts.text: French troops under Gaston de Foix rescue Bologna, which was under siege from a combined Papal-Spanish army
Beggars assault Oisterwijk in the Netherlands, drive nuns out
Connecticut passes the first US livestock branding law
Charlevoix Earthquake in Quebec, felt strongly in New England
Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I signs a peace treaty with France
American British Colony of Georgia becomes 1st US territory to abolish both entail and primogeniture land and estate inheritance practices
British garrison in Menorca (western Mediterranean) surrenders to French and Spanish fleet as part of the American Revolutionary War
Earthquakes ravage Calabria in Italy, killing 30,000
Sweden recognizes US independence
Zealand, Netherlands, surrenders to French general Michaud
English explorer George Bass and crew set sail from Sydney to Tahiti and Chile - they're never seen again
First US gas company incorporated in Baltimore (coal gas for streetlights)
Jean-Baptiste Bernadotte ascends to the thrones of Sweden and Norway
Hannah Lord Montague of NY creates the first detachable shirt collar
Jan van Speyk blows up his gunboat in Antwerp to avoid capture by the Belgian, killing about 30
Oregon Spectator is the first newspaper to be published on the West Coast
D.D. Parmelee patents the first key-driven adding machine in New Paltz, NY
Wallachia and Moldavia are united under Alexander John Cuza as the United Principalities
Kinematoscope patented by Coleman Sellers, Philadelphia
Louisiana delegation except Mr Bouligny withdraws from Congress (US Civil War)
Danish army begins its withdrawal from Danevirke to Dybbøl through driven snow, last time the Danes would occupy Danevirke, symbol of Danish identity
Federals occupy Jackson, Mississippi
US Civil War: Battle of Hatcher's Run, Virginia (Armstrong's Mill, Dabney's Mill)
World's largest alluvial gold nugget, the Welcome Stranger, is found by John Deason and Richard Oates (weighing 97.14 kg) in Moliagul, Australia
First motion picture shown to a theater audience airs in Philadelphia
Joseph Swan demonstrates light bulb using carbon glow
Phoenix, Arizona incorporates
News of the fall of Khartoum reaches London
Snow falls on San Francisco
Alfred Naess skates world record 500m (49.4 sec)
Female suffrage organization in Amsterdam forms
First showing of a motion picture in Hawaii takes place at the Hawaii Opera House
Marcel Proust meets Jean Lorrain in a pistol duel
British troops under Gen Buller occupy Vaal Krantz, Natal
First Loop-the-loop roller coaster opens on Coney Island, NY, built by Edwin Prescott
American occupation of Cuba ends
Society of Dutch Composers forms in Amsterdam
Congress overrides Wilson's veto, curtailing Asian immigration
Morosco Theater opens at 217 W 45th St NYC (demolished 1982)
Separation of church and state begins in USSR
Stephen W. Thompson becomes the first US pilot to down an enemy airplane
NL President John Heydler dismisses charges that Hal Chase bet against his team and threw games in collusion with gamblers
Yankees purchase 20 acres in Bronx for Yankee Stadium
Reader's Digest magazine 1st published
General mine strike against wage cuts in Saar Territory (previous part of Germany occupied by Allied forces after WWI)
Mass arrests of socialists & communists in Italy
Royal Greenwich Observatory begins broadcasting the hourly time signals known as the Greenwich Time Signal or the "BBC pips"
Jimmy Hatlo's "They'll Do It Every Time" cartoon debuts in San Francisco
5th Aliyah to Israel begins
Maxine Dunlap becomes 1st US women to earn a glider pilot license
American speed skater Jack Shea wins the 1,500m at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics; clinches sprint double after also taking out the 500m
Crew of Dutch "7 Provinces" mutiny after pay cuts
National Wildlife Federation forms
Silent film "Modern Times," directed by, written by and starringCharlie Chaplin, is released
Hans Engnestangen skates world record 500m (41.8 sec)
III British Empire Games open in Sydney, Australia
General Winckelman replaces Gen Reijnders as Dutch supreme commander
Dutch Premier De Geer returns from Lisbon to Netherlands
Braves get Tommy Holmes from Yanks for Buddy Hassett and Gene Moore
Amsterdam resistance group CS-6 shoots nazi general Seyffardt
Clandestine Radio Atlantiksender, Germany, 1st transmission
358 RAF bombers attack Stettin
Big Racket becomes fastest race horse at 69t.6 kph (440 yd/20.8 s)
The Chondoist Chongu Party is founded in North Korea
Bolewet Beirut becomes President of Poland
"Nature of Things" science show premieres on NBC prime time
Gretchen Fraser becomes 1st US woman Olympic ski slalom champion
Huaso sets official world equestrian high-jump record, 2.47 m, Chic
"Peter Pan", animated film by Walt Disney opens at Roxy Theater, NYC
Sweet rationing imposed in WWII ends in Britain
WCDC-TV channel 19 in Adams, Massachusetts (ABC) begins broadcasting
Finland goes 1-2 in the ski jumping at the Cortina d'Ampezzo Winter Olympics; Antti Hyvärinen takes gold ahead of teammate Aulis Kallakorpi
NYC Mayor Robert Wagner and Brooklyn Boro President Frank Cashmore sponsor a bill to create a $30M Brooklyn Sports Center Authority to build a new stadium for the Dodgers baseball team
VII Winter Olympic Games close in Cortina d'Ampezzo, Italy
Clifton R Wharton confirmed as 1st US African American foreign minister (to Romania)
Vanguard TV-3 back-up launches into Earth orbit; reaches 6 km

Black and White parents stage an overnight sit-in after school board denies transfer requests of nine Black families for their children to be transferred to newer school facility in Englewood, New Jersey
Sun, Moon, Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter and Saturn all within 16 degrees
Maarten Schmidt discovers enormous red shifts in quasars
Soviet lunar probe failure
Beursschouwburg opens in Brussels
BBC opens a relay radio station on Ascension Island
"The Smothers Brothers Comedy Hour" variety show premieres on CBS TV
Bollingen Prize for poetry awarded to Robert Penn Warren
A fishing trawler from Hull sinks off the coast of Iceland
KDTV TV channel 39 in Dallas-Fort Worth, Texas (IND) begins broadcasting
Skater Kees Verkerk wins olympic gold in the 1500m
"Turn-On," debuts and cancelled by ABC after flopping so badly
United States population reaches 200 million
Vince Lombardi, becomes part owner, vice-president, general manager, and head coach of NFL Washington Redskins
1st Test Cricket ton of Barry Richards, 126, 164 balls, 20 fours 1 six
Test Cricket debut of John Traicos, South Africa v Australia, Durban
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
WSCV TV channel 51 in Fort Lauderdale, FL (IND) suspends broadcasting
"Another Puff" by Jerry Reed peaks at #65
Bob Douglas is the first African American elected to the Basketball Hall of Fame
German speed skater Erhard Keller wins his 2nd consecutive Olympic 500m gold medal at the Sapporo Winter Games after taking out the event in Grenoble (1968)
Two IRA members are killed when a bomb they were planting exploded prematurely
US airlines begin mandatory inspection of passengers & baggage
Funeral held for US Army Lt. Col. William Nolde, last US soldier killed in Vietnam War
Juan Corona sentenced to 25 consecutive life terms for 25 murders
John Murtha of Pennsylvania becomes the first Vietnam War veteran elected to the United States Congress
Mats Wermelin, Sweden, scores all points in 272-0 basketball win
Maximum speed on Germany's Autobahn highway reduced to 100 kph
US Mariner 10 returns 1st close-up photos of Venus' cloud structure
"CB Savage" by Rod Hart peaks at #67
"Dis-Gorilla (part 1)" by Rick Dees peaks at #56
"Turn Loose On My Leg" by Jim Stafford peaks at #98
Comedy album "Up Your Nose" by Gabriel Kaplan peaks at #91
General Mills Adventure Theater premieres on CBS radio
Blizzard forms in US North East, 100 people killed in New England and New York over 3 days
Fred Newman makes 88 consecutive basketball free throws blindfolded
Costliest single periodical ad, $3.2 million, Gulf + Western in Time
Sears Radio Theater premieres on CBS
Egyptian parliament votes to end boycott of Israel
Largest Jell-O made in Brisbane with 9,246 gallons of watermelon-flavored gelatin
Military jury in North Carolina convicts Robert Garwood of collaborating with the enemy during the Vietnam War
British airline Laker Airways collapses owing 270M pounds ($351M)
DEA announces seizure of 3,192 tons of marijuana, 495 people
President of Suriname Hendrick Chin A Sen resigns and flees to the Netherlands
NZ beat England (82 & 93) by an innings in 3 days
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 2,200 for 1st time
Soyuz TM-2 launches
Arizona House of Reps vote to impeach Republican Governor Evan Mecham
Kareem Abdul-Jabar becomes 1st NBA player to score 38,000 points
Notre Dame becomes 1st team to sell its game to a major network (NBC)
All American Bowl ends after 14 years
Big East Football conference forms
LA King Dave Taylor becomes 29th NHler to score 1000 points
Last day of Test Cricket cricket for Dilip Vengsarkar
Mike Whitney career-best 7-27 at WACA in Test Cricket win v India
Grenade explodes in Sarajevo, killing 63 and injuring 160
R James Woolsey, becomes 16th director of CIA
"Where On Earth Is Carmen San Diego," debuts on Fox TV
68 killed and 200 wounded after a mortar bomb is set off in Sarajevo
Japan's Shinshinto Party wins local elections
Philip Glass' Symphony No. 3, for string orchestra, premieres with the Stuttgart Chamber Orchestra conducted by Dennis Russell Davies, in Künzelsau, Germany
British supermarket chains stock genetically modified tomato puree - the first GM food to be sold in the country
Japan's Ministry of Finance announces plans to cut import tariffs on crude oil and most petroleum products
Alberto Acciarito convicted of harassing his ex-wife Ingrid Rossellini
Author Tom Clancy confirms he signed agreement to purchase Minnesota Vikings for slightly more than $200 million, an NFL franchise record
Rebels from the Revolutionary Artibonite Resistance Front capture the city of Gonaïves, starting the 2004 Haiti rebellion
Twenty-three Chinese people drown when a group of 35 cockle-pickers are trapped by rising tides in Morecambe Bay, England. Twenty-one bodies are recovered.
Super Bowl XL, Ford Field, Detroit, MI: Pittsburgh Steelers beat Seattle Seahawks, 21-10; MVP: Hines Ward, Pittsburgh, WR
A major tornado outbreak across the Southern United States leaves at least 58 dead, the most since the May 31, 1985 outbreak that killed 88.
The United States Navy guided missile cruiser Port Royal runs aground off Oahu, Hawaii, damaging the ship as well as a coral reef
UK House of Commons votes in favour of same-sex marriage
King of the FranksLouis the Piousgrants the Archbishop of Salzburg immunity to secure loyalty from the clergy
KingAlfonso Vorders Sicily's Jews to attend conversion sermons
Roman Catholic GermanEmperor Maximilian Icaught in Belgium
KingHenry IIof France and Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vsign the Treaty of Vaucelles, creating a temporary truce in the Italian War of 1551–59
Henry of Navarre (laterHenry IV of France) abjures Catholicism at Tours
Puritan minister and founder of Rhode Island,Roger Williamsfirst arrives in Boston from England
Prince of Wales proclaimed KingCharles IIof Great Britain by Covenanter Parliament of Scotland
Isaac Newtonwrites to fellow polymathRobert Hooke“If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of Giants”
Methodist religious leadersJohn Wesleyand Charles Wesley arrive in Savannah, Georgia
Prince George, Prince of Wales, later George IV, is appointed as Prince-Regent after his father KingGeorge IIIis recognized as insane due to mental illness
Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg opens to the public withCatherine the Great's collection of 4,000 paintings forming its core collection
KingLeopold II of Belgiumestablishes the Congo as his personal colonial possession
Giuseppe Verdi's opera "Otello" premieres at La Scala Teatro in Milan, Italy, Verdi's first new opera for over 15 years
Draft of the Hay–Pauncefote Treaty to build the Panama Canal is sent to the US Senate by PresidentWilliam McKinley
Arnold Schoenberg's String Quartet No. 1 premieres in Vienna
Italian tenorEnrico Carusorecords "O Solo Mio" for the Victor Talking Machine Company in Camden, New Jersey
The last of the American troops commanded by GeneralJohn Pershingleave Mexico; President Carranza will be assassinated within the next year
Hollywood film studio United Artists founded byCharlie Chaplin,Douglas Fairbanks,Mary PickfordandD. W. Griffith
Mahatma Gandhiis released from Yerwada Prison in Pune, British India, on health grounds after a bout of appendicitis
"The General", American silent film starringBuster Keatonand Marion Mack, co-directed by Keaton and Clyde Bruckman, premieres in New York City
Malcolm Campbellsets world land speed record speed of 246.08 mph driving his famous Blue Bird car at Daytona Beach, Florida
Marinus van der Lubbepasses Dutch-German border, will later set fire to the German Reichstag
Harry Fergusonpatents the suction side hydraulic control system, allowing control of three-point mounted agricultural implements attached to tractors
FDRproposes enlarging Supreme Court, "court packing" plan failed
Glenn Millerand his Orchestra record "Tuxedo Junction"
"Woman of the Year" film directed by George Stevens, starringKatharine HepburnandSpencer Tracyopens at Radio City Music Hall, NYC

Jake LaMottadefeats future multi-weight world boxing championSugar Ray Robinsonby unanimous points decision in Detroit in the 2nd of their 6 meetings; his only win of their rivalry; Robinson’s first loss in his first 40 pro bouts
"Captain America" serial film premieres starring Dick Purcell, first appearance of a Marvel superhero outside of a comic book
18-year-oldDick Buttonbecomes first American men's figure skating Olympic gold medallist at the St. Moritz Winter Games; first to complete a double axel in competition; retains title in 1952
5th Emmy Awards: I Love Lucy, Thomas Mitchell andHelen Hayeswin
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 2nd Piano Concert
Gamal Abdel Nasseris nominated as the first President of the United Arab Republic
French PresidentCharles de Gaullecalls for Algeria's independence
Anastasio Somoza Debayleelected President of Nicaragua
Apollo 14, 3rd US manned Moon expedition, lands near Fra MauroAlan Shepard& Edward Mitchell (Apollo 14) walk on Moon for 4 hrs
"In The Mood" by Henhouse 5 Plus Too (Ray Stevens) peaks at #40
Future 5-division world boxing championSugar Ray Leonardmakes his professional debut with a 6-round unanimous decision over Luis Vega at Baltimore’s Civic Center
Joni Mitchellinducted into the Canadian Music Hall of Fame
Former Nazi Gestapo officialKlaus Barbieextradited to France from Bolivia to stand trial
ABC's "Nightline" airs interviews with Filipino presidential candidatesCorazon AquinoandFerdinand Marcos
1st prime-time network televised wrestling match in 30 years,André the GiantdefeatsHulk Hogan
Howard Sternkisses NFL New York Giants Leonard Marshall's derrière over bet Stern lost claiming the Giants would lose the Super Bowl
Michigan court bars Dr.Jack Kevorkianfrom assisting in suicides
Medgar Evers' murdererByron De La Beckwithsentenced to life, in Jackson, Mississippi, 30 years after the crime
Nancy KerriganandTonya Hardingtalk on FOX (Taped Dec 22nd)
Ed Sabol, co-founder of NFL Films, elected for enshrinement in the Pro Football Hall of Fame
Flavor FlavandElton Johnappear in a Pepsi Co. ad during Super Bowl XLVI
Super Bowl XLVI, Lucas Oil Stadium, Indianapolis, IN: New York Giants beat New England Patriots, 21-17; MVP:Eli Manning, New York, QB
A group of early Japanese Christians, known as the 26 Martyrs, are killed by the new government of Japan for being seen as a threat to Japanese society
Articles of Confederation ratified by 2nd state, South Carolina
1st Winter Olympic Games close at Chamonix, France
WWII: US troops under GeneralDouglas MacArthurenter Manila in the Philippines after a month-long battle, ending three years of Japanese military occupation
Jury selection begins in the trial of Los Angeles police charged with beatingRodney King
Sanjō, 67th Emperor of Japan (1011-16), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1017)
Aegidius Tschudi, Swiss statesman and historian known for "Chronicon Helveticum", born in Glarus, Switzerland  (d. 1572)
René of Chalon, Dutch Prince of Orange and Stadtholder of Holland, born in Breda, Duchy of Brabant (d. 1544)
Giovanni de' Bardi, Italian writer, soldier, composer, and patron of the arts (The Florentine Camerata), born in Republic of Florence (d. 1612)
Esteban Manuel de Villegas, Spanish poet, born in Matute, La Rioja, Spain (d. 1669)
Honorat de Brueil seigneur de Racan, French aristocrat playwright and poet, born in Aubigné-Racan, France (d. 1670)
Biagio Marini, Italian violin virtuoso and composer, born in Brescia, Republic of Venice (d. 1663)
Gaspar Schott, German Jesuit and scientist (cat piano), born in Königshofen, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1666)[1][2]
Marie de Rabutin-Chantal, marquise de Sévigné, French author famous for her letter-writing, born in Paris (d. 1696)
Giuseppe Vignola, Italian composer, born in Napoli, Habsburg Spain (d. 1712)
Gilbert Tennent, Irish Presbyterian religious leader in Colonial America, born in Vinecash, Ireland (d. 1764)
Joseph Umstatt, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Archduchy of Austria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1762)
James Otis, American lawyer and patriot, born in Barnstable, Massachusetts (d. 1783)
Nathanael Gottfried Gruner, German composer (Kantor at Gera), born in Zwickau, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1792)
John Jeffries, American physician and meteorologist (1st balloon flight across English Channel), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1819)
Christian Gottlob Neefe, German composer, conductor and tutor of Ludwig van Beethoven, born in Shimnitz, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1798)
Court Lambertus van Beyma, Frisian regent and leader of the Frisian patriots, born in Harlingen (d. 1820)
Alexandre Brongniart, French geologist and mineralogist (arranged geologic formations of the Tertiary Period), born in Paris, France (d. 1847)
Margaretha Jacoba de Neufville, Dutch author of "The Kidnap" - 1st Dutch historical novel, born in Amsterdam (d. 1856)
Nancy Hanks Lincoln, mother of Abraham Lincoln, born in Hampshire County, West Virginia (d. 1818)
Karoly Kisfaludy, Hungarian pioneering dramatist (A Tat rok Magyarorsz gon), born in Tét, Hungary (d. 1830)
Wilhelm Karl Ritter von Haidinger, Austrian physicist and mineralogist known for his work on optical appearances, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1871)
John Lindley, English botanist (Elements of Botany), born in Catton, England (d. 1865)
Johan Ludvig Runeberg, Finnish poet (Finland's national anthem "Maamme"), born in Jakobstad, Finland (d. 1877)
Carl Spitzweg, German painter (The Poor Poet, The Bookworm), born in Unterpfaffenhofen, Germany (d. 1885)
Ole Bull, Norwegian violin virtuoso and compsoer, born in Bergen, Norway (d. 1880)
Dwight Lyman Moody, American evangelist (Moody Church), born in Northfield, Massachusetts (d. 1899)
Hiram Maxim, American-British inventor (Maxim gun, 1st automatic machine gun), born in Sangerville, Maine (d. 1916)
John Boyd Dunlop, Scottish vet and inventor (pneumatic rubber tire), born in Dreghorn, Scotland (d. 1888)
Belle Starr [Myra Belle Shirley] American outlaw (American wild west), born in Carthage, Missouri (d. 1889)
Ignacio Carrera Pinto, Chilean hero of the War of the Pacific, born in Santiago, Chile (d. 1882)
Joris-Karl Huysmans, French writer (Against the Grain), born in Paris, France (d. 1907)
Terauchi Masatake, Japanese soldier and politician (Prime Minister of Japan 1916-18), born in Hirai Village, Japan (d. 1919)
Otto Brahm, German critic, theatre director (founded Neue Deutsche Rundschau), born in Hamburg (d. 1912)
Felipe Villanueva y Gutierrez, Mexican composer and violinist of the Porfiriato period, born in Tecámac, Mexico (d. 1893)
Armand Parent, Belgian violinist and composer, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1934)
Carl Teike, German military composer, born in Stettin-Altdamm, Pomerania (now Szczecin, Poland) (d. 1922)

Arthur Keith, Scottish anthropologist (human evolution), born in Persley Aberdeenshire (d. 1955)
Rossetter Gleason Cole, American composer (Heroes of Freedom), born in Clyde, Michigan (d. 1952)
Lodewijk Mortelmans, Belgian composer (Morgenstemming - Morning Mood), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1952)

Edward Rigby, British actor (Star Look Down, Young & Innocent), born in Ashford, Kent, England (d. 1951)
Frederick Lonsdale, British playwright (Balkan Princess), born in St Helier, Jersey (d. 1954)
Felice Lattuada, Italian composer and educator (Civica Scuola di Musica, Milan, 1935-62), born at Caselle di Morimondo, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1962)

Ernest Tyldesley, English cricket batsman (14 Tests, 3 x 100, 6 x 50, HS 138; Lancashire CCC), born in Worsley, England (d. 1962)

George Saiko, Austrian writer, born in Seestadtl, Bohemia (d. 1962)

Ivan Galamian, American violinist and violin teacher, born in Tabriz, Persia (d. 1981)

Pierre Pflimlin, French politician (briefly French Prime Minister 1958), born in Roubaix, France (d. 2000)
(Millicent) "Peg" Entwistle, British stage and screen actress, born in Port Talbot, Wales (d. 1932)[1]
Daisy and Violet Hilton, British conjoined twins, born in Brighton, England (d. 1969)
Charles Philippe Leblond, French-Canadian cell biologist known for his work on stem cell renewal, born in Lille, France (d. 2007)
Francisco Varallo, Argentine soccer inside-right (16 caps; Boca Juniors 222 games), born in La Plata, Argentina (d. 2010)
Willard Parker, American actor (Kiss Me Kate, What A Woman), born in New York City (d. 1996)

Ken Adam, British film production designer (James Bond sets), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2016)

Luc Ferrari, French composer,  in musique concrète and electroacoustic pioneer (Le Lever du jour au bord de la mer), born in Paris (d. 2005)

Cocky van Oost [Kommertje van Vliet], Dutch ballet dancer (Sylphides), and instructor, born in the Netherlands
Gaston Roelants, Belgian athlete (Olympic gold 3000m steeplechase 1964; WR 20k 57:44.4 & 1-hour race 20,878m 1972), born in Opvelp, Belgium

Larry Hillman, Canadian ice hockey defenceman (Stanley Cup 1955 Detroit RW, 1962, 63, 64, 1967 Toronto ML, 1969 Montreal Canadiens) and coach (Winnipeg Jets 1977-79), born in Kirkland Lake, Ontario (d. 2022)
Stuart Damon [Zonis], American stage and screen actor (General Hospital, 1977-2007 - "Dr. Alan Quartermaine"), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2021)
Wang Xuan, Chinese computer scientist, born in Wuxi, Jiangsu, China (d. 2006)
Arnaldo Gruarin, French rugby union prop (26 Tests; RC Toulon), born in Gruaro, Italy (d. 2025)
Dick van Niehoff, Dutch pop vocalist (Fouryo), born in the Netherlands (d. 2000)
John Guare, American playwright (6 Degrees of Separation, The House of Blue Leaves), born in New York City
Brian Luckhurst, English cricket batsman (21 Tests, 4 x 100, HS 131; Kent CCC), born in Sittingbourne, England
Derek Wadsworth, British jazz composer and arranger, born in Cleckheaton, Yorkshire, England (d. 2008)
Jane Bryant Quinn, newscaster/financial writer (Everyone's Money Book), born in Niagara Falls, New York
Patric Standford [né John Gledhill], British composer (Taikayoku; Christus Requiem; Toward Paradise), and educator, born in Barnsley, Yorkshire, England (d. 2014)[1]

H. R. Giger, Swiss surrealist artist (album cover art for ELP - "Brain Salad Surgery"; Debbie Harry - "'KooKoo"), and Oscar-winning special effects designer (Alien), born in Chur, Switzerland (d. 2014)

Barrett Strong, American R&B vocalist ("Money (That's What I Want)"), piano player, and songwriter ("I Heard It Through the Grapevine"; "Just My Imagination"; "Papa Was A Rollin' Stone"; "War"), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2023)[1]
David Selby, American actor (Falcon Crest; Flamingo Road), born in Morganstown, West Virginia
Henson Cargill, American country music singer ("Skip a Rope", "The Most Uncomplicated Goodbye I Ever Heard"), born in Oklahoma City (d. 2007)
Kaspar Villiger, Swiss tobacco manufacturer and politician, (Federal Councilor, 1989-2003), born in Pfeffikon, Switzerland
